title: Osteocyte-Intrinsic MMP13 Exacerbates Injury-Induced Temporomandibular Joint Osteoarthritis
research_question: Does osteocytic MMP13 play a causal role in temporomandibular joint osteoarthritis (TMJOA) progression, similar to its role in knee OA?
methods_summary: Generated DMP1-cre+/-;MMP13fl/fl mice with osteocyte-intrinsic MMP13 deletion
Induced TMJOA via intra-articular monosodium iodoacetate (MIA) injection in 12-week-old male mice
Evaluated TMJ and knee joint pathology using histology (Safranin-O/Fast Green, Ploton silver stain, Picrosirius red, TRAP stain, IHC/IF) and microCT
Performed statistical analysis using two-way ANOVA with post-hoc Tukey test
dataset: 16-week-old male DMP1-cre+/-;MMP13fl/fl mice and Cre-negative littermate controls (mixed FVB and C57BL/6 background); n=6-9 per group for TMJ studies, n=6-11 per group for knee studies
key_findings: Osteocytic MMP13 deficiency protected TMJ from MIA-induced cartilage degeneration and subchondral bone changes
MIA increased osteocyte lacunar density in control TMJs but not in DMP1-cre+/-;MMP13fl/fl TMJs
Osteocytic MMP13 deficiency prevented injury-induced subchondral bone plate thickening in both TMJ and knee
Baseline TMJ phenotype was unaffected by osteocytic MMP13 deletion, unlike knee which showed baseline OA
Collagen organization in TMJ subchondral bone was unchanged across all groups
extracted_insights: Osteocytic MMP13 is required for injury-induced TMJOA progression but not for TMJ homeostasis at baseline, contrasting with its role in knee OA where deficiency causes baseline degeneration. This highlights joint-specific differences in osteocyte-mediated bone-cartilage crosstalk and suggests that targeting osteocytic MMP13 could protect against TMJOA without affecting normal TMJ function.
